title: Variational Boundary Fluctuations as a First-Principles Origin of Langevin Noise
abstract: Stochastic forces are usually postulated or obtained by eliminating environmental degrees of freedom. Here we identify a variational origin: fluctuating endpoint data in Hamilton's principle induce fluctuations of the on-shell action. Hamilton--Jacobi propagation transports this boundary imprint, whose gradient generates an effective Langevin force inherited from boundary-action fluctuations. The resulting force is not freely specifiable: its amplitude is filtered by the Hessian of Hamilton's principal function, yielding multiplicative and state-dependent noise. Homogeneous additive Langevin forcing is recovered only as a Markovian coarse-grained limit.
summary: This paper proposes that Langevin noise is not an external input but emerges from fluctuations in the initial and final boundary conditions of a system's action. By using Hamilton-Jacobi theory, the author shows how these boundary uncertainties propagate to create a state-dependent effective force. This provides a first-principles link between deterministic variational mechanics and stochastic dynamics.
main_problem: The paper seeks to identify a first-principles, variational origin for Langevin noise, rather than treating it as a phenomenological or externally imposed stochastic force.
main_result: The author demonstrates that stochastic forces can emerge from fluctuations in Hamilton's principle boundary/endpoint data, resulting in a state-dependent, multiplicative noise that recovers standard additive Langevin noise only in the Markovian coarse-grained limit.
method: A variational framework using the first variation of the Lagrangian action and Hamilton-Jacobi propagation to transport boundary-induced perturbations through the bulk dynamics.
model_or_system: The study focuses on mechanical Hamiltonians of the form H(q, p) = p^2/2m + V(q), specifically using a harmonic oscillator as a benchmark, and discusses extensions to field theories.
key_observables: The effective Langevin force xi(t), which is found to be multiplicative, anisotropic, and state-dependent.
important_parameters: The Hessian of Hamilton's principal function (acting as a geometric filter), the overdamped limit, and the Markovian coarse-grained limit.
main_assumptions: Assumes a single-valued Hamilton-Jacobi branch and smooth boundary fluctuations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the distinction between fixed endpoints (a single reference extremal) and fluctuating endpoints (a family of nearby extremals due to environmental uncertainty).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of noise origins, develops a variational framework based on Hamilton's principle, uses Hamilton-Jacobi theory to derive the effective force, benchmarks the theory with a harmonic oscillator, and discusses limits and field-theoretic extensions.
why_interesting: This provides a fundamental derivation of stochasticity from deterministic variational principles, offering a new way to view the emergence of open quantum system dynamics and noise from boundary-driven uncertainty.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Variational origin of boundary-induced tra- jectory fluctuations. (A) With fixed endpoints, the admis- sible variational set A selects a reference extremal trajectory between states A and B, satisfying δA = 0. (B) Environmen- tal uncertainty in preparation and termination makes end- point constraints fluctuate, shown by red halos ηi, ηf. This selects neighbouring admissible shells, schematically repre- sented by e A, and generates a family of nearby extremals, shown as dashed trajectories.
